options.text: The electricity that powers a computer
Instructions written in a programming language that humans can read
The physical chips inside the computer

explanation_after: Source code is human-readable instructions. The computer cannot run it directly — it must be either compiled (translated all at once into machine code) or interpreted (translated line-by-line as it runs). Python is interpreted, which is one reason it feels fast to write but runs slower than compiled languages.

stem: Code executes in what order by default?

options.text: Randomly — whichever line seems most important
Top to bottom, left to right

explanation_after: Code runs top to bottom unless something explicitly changes the order — like a loop (repeats), a function call (jumps to another block), or a conditional (skips lines). Understanding this default order is foundational to reading any program.

stem: Why do AI systems still need code even if they 'learn' from data?

options.text: They don't — AI systems write themselves
Code defines the structure, training loop, and rules of what the AI is allowed to do
Code is only needed to connect AI to the internet
Code is only needed for older AI systems

explanation_after: An AI model learns WHAT to do from data, but the CODE defines HOW it learns — the architecture, training loop, loss function, data pipeline, and deployment. Even GPT-4 is thousands of lines of code wrapped around a trained model. Learning AI engineering means understanding both the code structure and the learning process.
